title: Optimal Transportation Theory
content:
This article is mainly based on the notes taken by Mr. Gu Xianfeng during his online class on transmission theory. There may be some clerical errors in it. This is caused by the pursuit of the efficiency of recording. At the same time, this article also contains some sentences excerpted from reading other related papers, which are not listed one by one for convenience. Finally, I sincerely thank Mr. Gu for his lectures.

¶Duality Theory
The duality theory was built first by Monge. After hundreds of years, Kantorovich made an important breakthrough, which earned him the Nobel Prize in Economics. His theory was later developed into the known Duality Theory. Brenier, who went a step further and reduced the problem to the Monge-Ampere equation, finally developed this theory into the Brenier theory.
In a nutshell, Monge poses the optimal transport map problem. But the solution of this problem does not necessarily exist, so Kantorovich reduced the problem condition so that the solution must exist and made it possible to be processed by linear program. The duality theory used the General Lagrange Multiplier to add the constraint into the energy and transformed it into a duality problem, which can be transformed into Euler Equation(PDE) by c-transform. Brenier finally transformed the PDE into Monge-Ampere equation.
There is a question left whether these questions are equal. Thus the first thing we should do is to prove the equivalence of the Kantorovich problem and the dual problem, which will be proved by cycle-monotonous theory. Brenier problem is actually a special case, we need to prove that the generalization of Brenier problem and Kantorovich problem are equivalent to each other in a sense., which will be proved by polar decomposition theory. The equivalence of the Kantorovich problem and the Monge problem will be proved by twisting condition. After proving the equivalence, we will further prove the stability of the solution and its generalization(Is the solution unique when relaxing the condition).
¶Monge Problem
We first make some assumptions about space. We assume X, Y are two complete (any cauchy sequence has a limit point) and separable or detachable(countable dense subsets exist). Given 2 probability measures, , where  denotes the infinate space composed of all probability measures in the  space, and cost function

It should be noted that the objective space is . The MP problem is:

Given a map  from , which may be smooth, continuous, or measurable. Is there exist another map You can't use 'macro parameter character #' in math modeT_# such that You can't use 'macro parameter character #' in math modeT_#\mu = \nu ? The answer to this question is yes, and there are infinitely many solutions to this problem. So we want to know if there is a best map.

Let’s do some further explanation. Given a(any) measurable subset , the total mass of the preimage is equal to the total mass of the image. That is:

Then we say You can't use 'macro parameter character #' in math modeT_#\mu = \nu.
How to define the best mapping? What we want is for each monocule  map to  there is a distance given by the cost function , and we want the total cost is the smallest, so we introduce a new concept: Transport Cost.

Monge’s problem is how to find the map that has the smallest  among all guaranteed measures maps, which is:
You can't use 'macro parameter character #' in math mode\mathbf{inf}{\int_X c(x, T(x))\mathbf d \mu(x): T_#\mu = \nu}
We want to know, whether the solution exists. If so, whether the solution is unique. If it is unique, how to find the solution? This question remained unresolved for hundreds of years. The central difficulty is:
You can't use 'macro parameter character #' in math mode\sum (\mu, \nu) := { T: X\rightarrow Y | T_# \mu = \nu}
is not closed, which means even though the function  decreases monotonically,  may not exist in the space. So we have to ask, what is a convergent mapping? We will thus introduce weak convergence.
Let us explain it from an economic point of view. We assume that  is the producer and  is the consumer. We want to deliver the produced goods to consumers, but the distribution of  and  is not uniform, some places produce more, and some places consume less. How do we design a transmission scheme that balances supply and demand? Of course, there are infinitely many such transmission schemes. How do we design the transmission method to minimize the freight, which is the optimal transmission problem.
We will define the Wasserstein Distance, which gives the total cost of optimal transport, as:
$$\mathcal{W}c(\mu, \nu) = \mathrm{inf}C(T)T# \mu = \nu\int_Y\gamma(x, y)\mathrm{d}y = (\pi_x)#\gamma = \mu\pi_x:X\rightarrow Y, (x, y)\mapsto x\pi_y: X\times Y\rightarrow Y, (x, y)\mapsto y$$
which also means $(\pi_y)#\gamma = \nu\gamma$.

We examine all joint probability distributions that satisfy
$$\pi(\mu, \nu) = {\gamma\in \mathcal{P}(X\times Y): (\pi_x)#\gamma = \mu, (\pi_y)#\gamma = \nu}.$$We call such a probability distribution a transmission scheme

We can clearly see that the transfer map is a special case of the transfer scheme, and the transfer scheme is a generalization of the transfer map. Thus we can see the transfer map as a special joint probability distribution , such that satisfied You can't use 'macro parameter character #' in math mode(\mathrm{id}, T)_#\mu. Generally the support is high-dimensional. Map is a causality while scheme is a correlation. Thus in machine learning, what many people are using is optimal transport. But if we learn the Brenier theory, we will find that the result we get is actually a transport map. It’s important to distinguish causality from correlation since the latter one will be more computationally expensive.
¶Kantorvich Theory
Actually,  is a convex space. That is, . Thus the space is also a simply connected space.

(KP) Given a transportation scheme, the cost is as follows:


We can see that this question is a linear one, for that:

and for the constraint, we get:

is also a linear one.
So this problem is transformed into a minima problem of finding a linear function on a convex set. We can find its extremum by translating the linear function, but it is not necessarily unique. And the theory can be naturally applied to both discrete and continuous cases, unlike traditional differential geometry. That is, for continuous cases, , it might be like:
$$ \left{  \right.  \left{  \right. $$
Now we need to prove the existence of the solution to Kantorvich Problem. The core idea of the proof is simple. We have a convex set , and linear functionals must find the maxima and minima on the convex set. But this convex set is an infinite-dimensional space in which we need to find a sequence of points that converges to a minima. The problem then boils down to how do we prove that this space is compact, how to define distances and Cauchy columns in the space, and how to prove that the Cauchy column converges and the limit of convergence is still in the space.

Def (Seperable Space): Topological Space X, if there exists a dense subset , such that  open set, .

For example, a rational number on the real axis is a dense set. Because any open set on the real axis must have a rational number.

Def (Compact): 

That is to say, Compact means that the space is closed for seeking the limit. The space of rational numbers is not compact, we can find rational numbers that approximate , but  is not a rational number.

Def (Norm): 


Def (Cauchy Sequence): 

In fact, the core skill of functional analysis is how to choose the norm. Different norm of the same set will lead to different convergence.

Def (Complete):  Cauchy Sequence , , and .

For example, the open set  is noncomplete, while the closed set  is complete. The difficulty of the Monge problem is that the admissible solution space is noncomplete.
Taken together, our requirements for space are linear, normed, complete, which is the Banach Space. There are two kinds of convergence in Banach space, strong convergence and weak convergence. Strong convergence means that the Cauchy column converges in Banach space. Sometimes the meaning of this convergence is too strong, so we introduce weak convergence.

Def (Dual Space):  is a vector space, dual space 

It can be shown that the dual of a dual space is equal to itself when the space dimension is finite. Dual spaces are introduced to define generalized functions. Sometimes it is difficult to study the convergence of the original space, but it converges in the dual space. We denote 

Def (Weak Convergence): Given a Banach Space and a sequence  weakly converge to , denoted as , iff  , we have .

That is to say, we examine the weak convergence of a sequence, and examine whether the effects of their actions on the dual space are the same. Similarly, we can also study the convergence of a functional sequence.

Def (Weak - Convergence): Given a Banach Space and a sequence  weakly converge to , denoted as , iff  , we have .


Theorem (Banach - Alaoglu):  is a Separable Banach Space, , 

In other words, dual space of separable Banach spaces is compact.

Def (lower - semi - continous): Given a metric space , ,  convergent sequence , we have 

SImilarly we can define upper-semi-continous:

Def (upper - semi - continous):  continuous   lower simi continuous and  upper semi continuous


Def ():  is a metrix space,  is continuous, euqals to 0 as ,

 is a confusing concept. In another words,  if and only if  and ,  compact , , . We can define a norm on , that is:
$$||f||\infty := \underset{x\in X}{\mathrm{sup}} |f(x)|C_b(x) := {f\in C(x): |f(x)| \leq M, \forall x\in X, \exists M}$$
 under $||\cdot||\infty$ is compact metric space.

Def (Signed measure): Given a metric space ,  is a signed finite measure.  Borel set ,  assigns a real number ,   countable disjoint union , , we haveand 
  := 

We can generate the positive signed measure as:

Def(Positive Signed Measure): ,



Def (Probability Measure): ,  is a probability measure means that


 ,  is dual to  ().

title: Graph Representations Overview
content:
The original intention of writing this article is to record my own progress in reading the paper when I was conceiving of a transfer learning graph embedding thesis. The update of this article is based on real-time paper reading. This article records the key steps and is not for all readers. More functions are for myself own memo. Therefore, some of the text is directly taken from the author’s paper.

¶Wasserstein Weisfeiler-Lehman Graph Kernels
Paper Wasserstein Weisfeiler-Lehman Graph Kernels(cited 110 times) proposed the graph Wasserstein distance and how kernels can be derived from it, and also proposed a seemly out-of-date node embedding method. Thus, above all, this paper’s main contribution is propose a kernel function over graphs by directly comparing non-parametric node embeddings as point cloudsthrough optimal transport (Wasserstein distance).
¶Generate Node Embedding
The specific node embedding method depends on whether the representation of a node is continuous according to its labels or features (the authors seem to be a little confused on these two distinctions). For the labelled non-attributed graph:
$$\ell{h+1}(v)=\operatorname{hash}\left(\ell{h}(v), \mathcal{N}^{h}(v)\right)a{h+1}(v)=\frac{1}{2}\left(a{h}(v)+\frac{1}{\operatorname{deg}(v)} \sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}(v)} w((v, u)) \cdot a^{h}(u)\right)X_{G}{h}=\left[x{h}\left(v_{1}\right), \ldots, x{h}\left(v_{n_{G}}\right)\right]{T},$$
where  $x{h}(\cdot)=\ell{h}(\cdot)x{h}(\cdot)=a{h}(\cdot)X_{G}^{h} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{G} \times m}GhGH$
¶Compute Wasserstein distance
The summary of graph Wasserstein distance is shown as follows:

For categorical node features, normalised Hamming distance is used:
$$d_{\text {Ham }}\left(v, v^{\prime}\right)=\frac{1}{H+1} \sum_{i=1}^{H+1} \rho\left(v_{i}, v_{i}^{\prime}\right), \quad \rho(x, y)=\left{\right.d_{E}\left(v, v{\prime}\right)=\left|v-v{\prime}\right|_{2}$$
¶Compute Wasserstein Graph Kernel


Def (Wasserstein Weisfeiler-Lehman): Given a set of graphs  Missing or unrecognized delimiter for \left\mathcal{G}=\left{G_{1}, \ldots, G_{N}\right}  and the GWD defined for each pair of graph on their WL embeddings, the Wasserstein Weisfeiler Lehman (WWL) kernel is defined as

title: Effective Python
content:
This article is used to improve Python programming skills, based on some summaries when reading the book Effective Python, and is still being updated (because the book is still being read slowly). In the past, I didn’t have many skills in Python programming, and most of my code followed some habits of C++, which led to the sometimes too verbose code written. This blog records some common operations or the extraction of confusing concepts during reading. And also includes some code used in my own testing.

¶Bytes and Str
Python has two types that can represent sequences of characters, bytes and str. A bytes instance contains raw data, an 8-bit unsigned value. We can directly use ASCII encoding to replace any character we need.
For example:
a = b'h\x65llo'print(list(a))print(a)
The output is:
[104, 101, 108, 108, 111] b'hello'

We can easily find that the ASCII value corresponding to e is 65, which is why the second output is ‘hello’. The first line is the hex form of the corresponding ASCII values. For example, we can transform 65 into the hex form, which is actually 101. Using the toolkit in this website is quite convient to achieve this goal: https://www.rapidtables.com/convert/number/decimal-to-octal.html

While the instances of str contain Unicode code points (also called code points) that correspond to text characters in human languages. For example:
a = 'a\u0300 propos'print(list(a))print(a)
The output is:
['a', '̀', ' ', 'p', 'r', 'o', 'p', 'o', 's'] à propos
Unicode encoding can generate a lot of special characters. To convert Unicode data to binary data, the encode method of str must be called. To convert binary data to Unicode data, the decode method of bytes must be called. When calling these methods, you can clearly indicate the encoding scheme you want to use, or you can use the system default scheme, usually UTF-8.

Let’s take a look at the specific code to understand the conversion of str and bytes:
a = "T-shirt"b = a.encode("utf-8")c = a.encode("gbk")print(a == b, a == c, b == c)
The output is:
False False True
It seems that different encoding method doesn’t make any difference. However, when we replace a with Chinese or other types of text, we will see the difference:
a = "T恤"b = a.encode("utf-8")c = a.encode("gbk")print(a == b, a == c, b == c)
The output is:
False False False
When we print a, b, c separately, we will see the following outputs:
T恤 b'T\xe6\x81\xa4' b'T\xd0\xf4'
That is exactly the difference between different encoding method.
The above conversion between str and bytes is for text content. If there are other binary content (such as pictures), bytes cannot be decoded into str. See the exception of the following code:
img = open('str-bytes.jpg', 'rb').read()img.decode('utf8')
It would raise UnicodeDecodeError
We can write a helper function to facilitate our conversion of bytes and str.
def to_str(bytes_or_str):    if isinstance(bytes_or_str, bytes):        result = bytes_or_str.decode("utf-8")    else:        result = bytes_or_str    return resultprint(repr(to_str(b"foo")))print(repr(to_str("stupid")))
The output is:
'foo' 'stupid'
Similarly, we can define a to_bytes function:
def to_bytes(bytes_or_str):    if isinstance(bytes_or_str, str):        result = bytes_or_str.encode("utf-8")    else:        result = bytes_or_str    return result  print(repr(to_bytes(b"foo")))print(repr(to_bytes("stupid")))
The output is:
b'foo' b'stupid'
What we need to pay attention to is that the bytes type and str type cannot do the same kind of operation. For example, when we perform the following concatenation operation:
b"foo" + "s"
A TypeError will raise.
Determining whether the bytes and str instances are equal always evaluates to False, even if the two instances represent exactly the same characters, they are not equal. For example, in the following example, the strings they represent are equivalent in ASCII encoding.
print(b"foo" == "foo")
The output will be False. At the same time, instances of both types can appear on the right side of the % operator to replace the %s in the format string on the left. However, if the string is of type bytes, then %s cannot be replaced with an instance of str, because Python does not know what scheme the str should be encoded in. But the reverse is possible, that is, if the format string is of type str, you can replace the %s in it with a bytes instance. The problem is that this may not be the same as what you want.
print(b"you are %s" % b"foo")print("you are %s" % "intersting")print(b"you are %s" % "foo")print("you are %s" % b"intersting")
The outputs of these statements, in turn, are:
b'you are foo' you are interstingTypeError: %b requires a bytes-like object, or an object that implements __bytes__, not 'str'you are b'intersting'
In addition, if the ‘w’ mode is specified when calling the open function, the system will require that it must be written in text mode. If you want to use binary mode, you should specify ‘wb’. In text mode, the write method accepts str instances containing Unicode data, not bytes instances containing binary data. So, we have to change the mode to ‘wb’ to fix the problem.
¶Formatted String
Using the % operator to stuff values into a C-style format string has many problems and is cumbersome to write. Just like:
templete = "%s loves food. See %s cook"name = "Max"formatted = template % (name, name)print(formatted)
Actually, there are three main troubles using the C-style format string:

If the values in the tuple on the right of % change in type or order, the program may fail due to incompatibility when converting types;
Before filling in the template, it is often necessary to do a little processing of the value to be filled in. But in this way, the whole expression may be written very long, which makes people feel more confusing;
If you want to fill multiple positions in the format string with the same value, you must repeat the value correspondingly multiple times in the tuple to the right of the % operator.

The str.format method uses a set of mini-languages to define its format specifiers that give us some useful concepts, but in other respects the method is as diverse as C-style format strings disadvantages, so we should also avoid using it. The example is as follows:
template = (    "you are {adj}, "    "your {noun} is beautiful")formatted = template.format(adj="clever", noun="hair")print(formatted)
THe output is:
you are clever, your hair is beautiful
f-string adopts a new way of writing, which fills the value into the string, which solves the biggest problem caused by the C-style format string. f-strings is a concise and powerful mechanism for embedding arbitrary Python expressions directly in format specifiers. The exmple is as follows:
key = "why you stay here?"value = "because of you"formatted = f"John: {key}\nLucy: {value}"print(formatted)
The output is:
John: why you stay here? Lucy: because of you
¶Unpacking
Unpacking is a widely used mechanism in Python, and any iterable object can be split, no matter how many layers of iterative structures there are in it. The writing method of Unpacking allows us to assign the elements in the tuple to multiple variables with only one statement:
string1, string2 = ("this is the first string for turple", "this is another string")print(string1)
A great effect of this writing is that we can swap two variables in place through unpacking without having to create temporary variables. As an example, for the simplest bubble sort, our traditional implementation looks like this:
def bubble_sort(a):    for i in range(len(a)):        for j in range(1, len(a)):            if a[j-1] < a[j]:                temp = a[j-1]                a[j-1] = a[j]                a[j] = temp    return ato_sort_list = [2, 4, 6, 3, 12, 6, 2, 3, 432, 1]bubble_sort(to_sort_list)
The result is:
[432, 12, 6, 6, 4, 3, 3, 2, 2, 1]
However, using the unpacking mechanism, we can simplify the code as:
def bubble_sort(a):    for i in range(len(a)):        for j in range(1, len(a)):            if a[j-1] < a[j]:                a[j], a[j-1] = a[j-1], a[j]    return ato_sort_list = [2, 4, 6, 3, 12, 6, 2, 3, 432, 1]bubble_sort(to_sort_list)
The result is of course the same as before.
¶Enumerate
It is recommend that we should replace range with enumerate as much as possible. Let’s start with an example. We have a list and we want to print the position of the element in the list while looping to print each element. Using the formatted string trick mentioned earlier, we can write code like this:
food_list = ["banana", "apple", "chicken", "noddle"]for i in range(len(food_list)):    print(f"{i}: {food_list[i]}")
The output is:
0: banana 1: apple 2: chicken 3: noddle
In fact, Python has a built-in function enumerate that can do this more concisely. Enumerate can wrap any iterator into a lazy generator:
it = enumerate(food_list)print(next(it))print(next(it))
The output is:
(0, 'banana') (1, 'apple')
Each pair of data output by enumerate can be unpacked into the two variables of the for statement, which will make the code clearer. The starting sequence number can be specified by the second parameter of enumerate (default is 0):
for i, flavor in enumerate(food_list, 1):    print(f"{i}, {flavor}")
The output is:
1, banana 2, apple 3, chicken 4, noddle
¶Zip
The zip function wraps two or more iterators into a lazy generator. Each time it loops, it fetches the next element from each of these iterators and puts those values in a tuple. For example:
names = ["Andy", "Louis", "Sheley", "Chicken"]names_len = [len(i) for i in names]for name, name_len in zip(names, names_len):    print(f"{name}'s length is {name_len}")
The output is:
Andy's length is 4 Louis's length is 5 Sheley's length is 6 Chicken's length is 7
If the lengths of the lists entered into the zip are inconsistent, the number of loops is actually equal to the length of the shortest list. Because the design of the zip function determines that as soon as any one of the iterators is processed, it will stop running.
If we want to traverse the longest string, instead of stopping at the short one, we can use the zip_longest function built in the itertools module:
import itertoolsnames = ["Andy", "Louis", "Sheley", "Chicken"]names_len = [len(i) for i in names]names.append("OhNoMatchName")for name, name_len in itertools.zip_longest(names, names_len):    print(f"{name}'s length is {name_len}")
The output is:
Andy's length is 4 Louis's length is 5 Sheley's length is 6 Chicken's length is 7 OhNoMatchName's length is None
¶for … else …
Python’s loops have a feature that most programming languages don’t support, namely the ability to place an else block immediately after the entire loop structure.
The logic of for ... else or while ... else is that if the code in the for loop jumps out early, the code in the else will not be run. If the code in the loop runs normally, the code in the else will also run. Let’s consider an example, we can use this structure when doing prime number determination. First try the numbers that can divide them at the same time one by one, if you still can’t find such a number after trying all of them, then the loop will be executed from the beginning to the end (this means that the loop does not jump out ahead of time because of break), and then the program will execute the else block.
a = 4b = 9for i in range(2, min(a, b)+1):    print("Testing", i)    if a % i == 0 and b % i == 0:        print("Not coprime")        breakelse:    print("Coprime")

title: 和茜茜去云南
content:
假期去云南，这个计划是和茜茜在120（宿舍楼）楼下的老地方“架膝长谈”时候花了三分钟定的。茜茜当时还在准备着考试，而我毕业季也忙的很，计划一直拖到出发前两天才匆匆定下，这也导致了前两天我们踩了一些坑。但和茜茜出去旅游最棒的一点就是，俩人都很有默契，即使是旅途中有多多少少的差强人意，我们也能迅速调整好重新出发。我们最后的行程与最初的计划截然不同，但回想起这七天，实在是一场很棒的旅行~

¶行程安排
第一天：广州飞机到昆明（昆明没什么玩的，可以直接中转去其他地方），若实在有空可以下飞机后中转到海洪湿地公园划小船，提前查询时间段内是否有海鸥，若有就到滇池喂海鸥（不要坐索道上去，滇池就在下面，千万不要去西山公园和茶马花街，特别无聊），若在昆明呆一个晚上，可以住在南强路附近，那里有小吃街，生活气息比较浓，而且第二天坐高铁去丽江或大理方便。
第二天：下午高铁前往大理，可以感受晚上11点之后古城内路边青年民谣喝酒摆摊的氛围，这是丽江和独克宗都没有的。当天晚上租电动车或敞篷车准备第二天环洱海，具体的路线直接问租车的老板，会介绍的很详细。若租电动车一定要租锂电池的（200公里及以上的），价格大约100元，建议直接高德地图找康辉旅游租电动车。古城里的酒吧基本点一杯酒都是100左右，可以去大冰的小屋体验一下，较实惠而且氛围很好。大理古城里外卖的吃的都不咋好吃，没啥必要买。记得旺季吃午饭提前预约一下，看看美团排名前几的几家菌锅都不错，服务也很好，比如清宏私厨、洱月小厨、源汁源味等，都挺不错的。住宿可以住在大理梵居栖溪汀苑民宿，这家店是在古城外面，但环境很好，晚上可以在院子拍拍照，可以住那个大一点的房型，价格贵的不算太多，体验很好。
第三天：（骑电动环洱海版，租敞篷车问问老板）早上可以睡晚一点，大约11点吃完饭开始环洱海，直接从大理往喜洲古城去，大约20公里，中间会经过磻溪村（网红S弯），可以骑进去逛逛拍几张照片，但不用呆太久，因为环海西路主要就是骑单车玩的，租电动或者租车没必要呆太久，没必要租自行车拍照，看起来太蠢。可以买帽子，记得砍价（我们买的是30元2顶，价格还可以啦）。喜洲古城可以逛逛，那里的东西稍微便宜一些，去大麦田那拍拍照，路边的咖啡店也可以拍拍照，咖啡不知道多少钱，但觉得没有必要。附近有些扎染体验店，可以看看别人怎么搞的，但自己尝试就没多大必要，我们试了一个85块钱扎染袋子的，流程就是选一下想染的图案，用线扎起来，然后他会浸到板蓝根水里染色，然后就完事了，体验一般。随后往环海东路的双廊古城骑，那边也可以逛逛，但也都差不多，双廊古城如果车骑不进去就抬进去，问题不大没人查的，可以骑出来。环海东路可以好好拍照，一路风景都很美，中间可以下车去近海的位置拍拍照，有些地方甚至可以下到海边，有树有海有阳光风景很棒。其实环洱海最美的就是路上，而不是某个景区，所以不要对那些目的地节点抱太高的期望，享受路上就好。下一站可以在小普陀停一下看看，其实就是一条延到海里的小路，还可以但也就那样。然后去理想邦，理想邦如果能在下午快落日的时候去就最好了，那里很出片，多拍拍照。然后就往洱海公园那边走，这个时候应该是黄昏，路边多停下拍拍照，这个时候应该是最舒服的时候了。然后就一直往酒店的方向导航就成。晚上逛逛古城吃点东西，早点回去休息，应该会比较累。
第四天：前往丽江，丽江古城若有行李建议住在外面，古城里的石板路拖行李比较麻烦，而且不方便打车等交通方式，若住古城里面记得提前问老板能不能接。建议到了丽江古城体验一下盲人按摩，手法非常不错。丽江古城卖的东西相比起其他便宜很多，可以尝尝鲜花饼和云南小粒咖啡和果酒等。去酒吧记得别太晚，太晚很多都关门或者不唱歌了，就没意思了。丽江吃的东西都很便宜，直接美团搜一下就好，我们呆的时间不是很久所以没有太多建议。
第五天：丽江前往香格里拉（直接坐客运车过去），香格里拉就住松赞林寺附近可以，也可以住古城里面。下午可以去松赞林寺走走，然后晚点就坐大巴环一下纳帕海（2元1位），没必要租车环或骑马，有些时候水位会很高，很有意思，但租电动车环就很惨。晚上就逛逛独克宗古城，那里的环境和其他古城还是有区别的，可以编个彩辫也可以找个旅拍拍一下照，下午七点四方街那大家都在一起跳舞，可以一起去跳一下，大转轮那晚上可以拍拍照，还蛮好看的，但藏族人认为这是盗版的，不要太当真就好。第二天如果想去巴拉格宗可以报个团，比较便宜，当日来回。我们第二天去的是德钦雨崩村，所以没去巴拉格宗。
第六天：如果不去德钦雨崩村的话，可以去附近的玉龙雪山（提前查还有没有雪），因为这次7月份去，玉龙雪山没雪了，我们就去德钦的梅里雪山，住的话如果不准备去雨崩村徒步可以住在飞来寺，那边交通挺方便，直接从香格里拉坐客运车就能到，第二天运气好可以看到日照金山，提前定好闹钟，定好可以直接看到雪山的酒店。
第七天：如果前一天住在雨崩村，可以在雨崩村徒步。进出雨崩村直接坐越野车，不要徒步，200到上雨崩，260到下雨崩，回程上雨崩150，雨崩村的住宿和吃饭价格都相对较高而且质量一般，要有预期。徒步有三天线路，神瀑、神湖、冰湖，难度递减，期望不要太高，神瀑的路线相对更成熟。记得在酒店免费借登山杖，不用自己带。具体路线的攻略自己查就行，网上很多。需要注意的是记得穿多一点，带多点水，走快一点，多问路。住宿如果图方便最好住下雨崩，上雨崩交通便宜一点，但相对要走的多一些（去徒步需要经过下雨崩，从上雨崩到下雨崩得走40分钟左右，路比较难走而且比较累，越野车到下雨崩价格比上雨崩贵60块），如果在雨崩村多住几天可以尝试两三条线路，下午可以在下雨崩喝个咖啡，可以骑个马什么的，但价格要有预期。
¶注意事项

环洱海和很多地方都记得涂防晒，套冰袖
云南早晚温差大，外出记得带外套，厚薄看个人，如果骑电动环洱海更得带，不然冷死
很多路程要么拼车要么坐客运大巴，拼车提前联系好司机，直接找客栈老板要司机电话，然后找司机问有没有拼车的，或者让他给介绍。如果从德钦回香格里拉价格80-100一人，出了雨崩村直接定就好。实际上可以电话里联系好一辆车，现场看到有车就直接上（虽然有点不道德，但可以避免电话里联系的车需要等别人太久，司机很会说大话的，也可以避免现场没车），从德钦客运站到雨崩村坐越野车点可以砍价到大约120一辆车，很难拼到车，时间充裕也可以坐客运大巴过去，自己看时间怎么衔接就好
环洱海不要报团，自己去就成
住洱海边要小心蚊子，提前问问老板和当地人，要住的话可以住在龙龛码头，看日出什么的好看，看日落可以去才村码头，如果有时间的话
去雨崩村的越野车两个人的话坐中间最好，不要坐最后一排，坐到吐而且没什么好看的，第一排只能坐一个人。另外等车时间比较长，如果赶车需要提前预留好时间提前到！

¶彩蛋

雨崩村徒步路线下山点有一家客栈，当时我们吃的松茸特别便宜（可能是运气好而已，不一定可复制），那边是直接采松茸的地方，可以在那买点松茸
当地人都特别好，有不懂的多问人，想了解路线更详细的信息找当地的旅行社多问问，报团就慎重，因为很多没什么必要抱团，除非太远的地方
泸沽湖比较远，但风土人情有意思而且天气好的话很美，可以在那住一个晚上
西双版纳没去，下次准备去
一些其他可选景点：昆明石林、香格里拉虎跳峡等等
香格里拉风景最好，路上少睡觉多看看窗外拍照

¶咔咔咔

在云南的路上随处可以见到大大小小的牛躺在路中间，车不得绕道而行，牛就甩着尾巴斜眼瞥着人，好不牛气！

我们到达香格里拉第一站就去了纳帕海，我们叫了个滴滴环湖一周，实际上有环湖巴士的，我们也是之后才知道。记得去纳帕海千万不要骑电动车，因为水位可能比你想象中的要高很多很多，我们开着车过去司机都说喇叭进水了哑了

在纳帕海边茜茜被海风捧起的头发

我们住在雨崩村猫在客栈，价格挺贵，但确实窗外风景很美，老板找不到钥匙还给我们升级了超级房型！但雨崩村那吃的真的又不好又贵~~~

我们去神瀑路上人们一直和我们说不要再走啦，不要再走啦，再走天黑啦回不来啦，但我们只在那呆一个下午，拖着也得走完呀！茜茜特别棒，一路都没有喊累没有喊疼，回到酒店脚都起水泡了，疼了好几天！

我们四点出发，经历了多次走岔路，到了七点二十终于到达了神瀑，虽然风景没有预想中的美，但沿路的经历实在难忘，最后我们喝了一口神瀑的水，希望未来一年能健健康康万事顺遂！

捕捉一个在艰难走路的茜茜

在香格里拉第一站的独克宗古城，下午七点的四方街人们翩翩起舞


在大理的第三天，我们应茜茜的强烈要求去了彩虹农庄，这里真的很出片，老板人也挺热情，一直给我们拍照，这几张拍的真的像蜜月照，带上茜茜拍照以后都压根不用找旅拍，太美了~
